title: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Monitoring
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.2` result set, dashboard panel, and archived audit evidence export for NIS2 Supply Chain Security Monitoring in `index=audit_evidence` with RFC3161 timestamp metadata when exported.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(d) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.2: NIS2 Supply Chain Security Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.2' running on sourcetype cyberark:session and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Insert fixture rows where `target_domain` resolves through `threat_intel_domain_lookup` with non-null weight and `total_min` exceeds 120; the saved search returns the supplier session with user, target_host, and session counts within the same hour bucket as the fixture timestamp.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Load fixture traffic for a maintenance-approved supplier jump host listed in `vendor_pam_cutover_exceptions.csv` with a future expiry; the scheduled search completes with zero result rows and the audit log records the suppression lookup hit.

dataSources: `index=pam` `sourcetype=cyberark:session` normalized fields user, target_host, session_id; optional correlation to `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:cmdb_rel_ci` for supplier CMDB relationships via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)); optional exposure context from `index=vuln` `sourcetype=qualys:hostdetection` (HOSTNAME, QID, CVE_ID) via Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964).
app: Splunk Add-on for CyberArk ([Splunkbase 2891](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2891)), Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)) for `threat_intel_domain_lookup`; Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) when CMDB joins are required.
spl:
index=pam sourcetype="cyberark:session" earliest=-24h
| rex field=target_host "(?<target_domain>[a-zA-Z0-9][a-zA-Z0-9.-]+\.[a-zA-Z]{2,})$"
| stats sum(duration_min) as total_min, dc(session_id) as sessions by user, target_host, target_domain, target_account
| lookup threat_intel_domain_lookup domain AS target_domain OUTPUT description AS ti_description, weight AS ti_weight
| where isnotnull(ti_weight) OR total_min>120
| sort - total_min
description: Flags privileged vendor and supplier jump sessions when the landing domain matches a curated threat-intelligence watchlist or when a single operator spends an unusually long time in one supplier session, using CyberArk PSM session fields as the primary signal.
value: Reduces guesswork after a supplier breach headline: you get a short, dated list of who touched which supplier systems and whether those domains were already on your intelligence radar, so legal and procurement can prioritize calls without waiting for spreadsheets from four different teams.
implementation: (1) Install Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (2891) and confirm Vault/PSM logs land in `index=pam` with `sourcetype=cyberark:session`. (2) In Enterprise Security (263), export or sync `threat_intel_domain_lookup` from Intelligence Downloads or your STIX/TAXII workflow. (3) Maintain `vendor_asset_lookup` / CMDB-driven tags so `target_host` can be tied to a supplier record. (4) Save the SPL as a scheduled alert with a 24 hour window and throttle on `user,target_host`.
visualization: Table (sessions with TI hits, total_min, sessions), bar chart of minutes by supplier_domain, heatmap of user by hour-of-day.
knownFalsePositives:
• **CyberArk PSM cutover or vault migration** — Distinguish: many new targets and longer sessions during an approved change with a ServiceNow CHG record. Suppress: time-bound exception rows in `vendor_pam_cutover_exceptions.csv` keyed on target_host with expiry_epoch.
• **Threat intelligence stale or duplicated domains** — Distinguish: TI shows a match but the intel source timestamp is older than 30 days. Suppress: add `where ti_last_seen > relative_time(now(),"-30d@d")` after enriching lookup outputs or refresh feeds under Configuration > Data inputs > modular inputs for your ES threat list.
• **Break-glass contractors covering multiple suppliers** — Distinguish: one shared CyberArk user legitimately rotates across many targets weekly. Suppress: lookup `shared_vendor_breakglass.csv` for allowed user,target_domain pairs and filter those rows out.
• **Red-team or penetration supplier infrastructure** — Distinguish: DNS matches a supplier lab stamped in `pentest_allowlist.csv`. Suppress: join project_id from the lookup before firing the alert.
• **Long sessions onbastion-only internal zones** — Distinguish: target_host stays inside a documented `.corp` bastion pattern with no outbound supplier data path. Suppress: `where NOT match(lower(target_domain),"\\.corp\\.supplier\\.int$")` or maintain a suffix allow list lookup.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC implements NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) — supply chain security, including security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers. It monitors the network boundary between the entity and its suppliers by analysing traffic patterns, detecting anomalous data volumes, and correlating with the supplier risk register.
• **Delineation from UC-22.2.16, UC-22.2.25, and UC-22.2.50**: UC-22.2.16 monitors supplier network traffic volume and assessment SLA compliance. UC-22.2.25 tracks supplier assessment coverage and Dependabot alerts. UC-22.2.50 provides deep Qualys/SBOM/PAM analysis per supplier. This UC (22.2.2) focuses on real-time supply chain network traffic monitoring — detecting when a supplier's traffic pattern changes significantly. It serves as the network-layer early warning for supply chain compromise.
• **Data sources**:
  - `index=firewall sourcetype=pan:traffic` or equivalent — firewall traffic logs showing source/destination IPs, ports, bytes, application identification. The Palo Alto TA (Splunkbase 2757) is the reference, but any CIM-compliant Network_Traffic source works.
  - `nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv` — lookup mapping supplier IP ranges to supplier names. Columns: `cidr` (IP range in CIDR notation, e.g., "10.200.0.0/24"), `supplier_name`, `supplier_tier`, `contract_id`, `allowed_ports` (comma-separated list of permitted destination ports, e.g., "443,8443,22"), `allowed_apps` (comma-separated Palo Alto application IDs, e.g., "ssl,ssh").
  - `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:change_request` — ServiceNow change requests for supplier network changes. Used to correlate traffic anomalies with approved change tickets.
• **Supplier network zones**: ideally, each critical supplier has a dedicated firewall zone or VLAN. Traffic from supplier zones can be monitored independently. If suppliers share a generic zone, use the IP range lookup for identification.
• **Baseline and anomaly detection**: the UC establishes a 30-day rolling baseline of traffic volume (bytes in/out) per supplier. A deviation of > 200% from the baseline triggers an anomaly alert. This detects: data exfiltration from the entity via a compromised supplier connection, unexpected data staging by a supplier, or a new application/protocol that hasn't been authorised.
• **RBAC**: role `nis2_supply_chain` with `srchIndexesAllowed = firewall, itsm, audit_evidence`.
• **Index retention**: `index=firewall` ≥ 90 days (network forensics). `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Firewall traffic data**: already configured for general network monitoring. Verify CIM compliance:
```
| tstats count from datamodel=Network_Traffic where earliest=-1h by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.dest_port All_Traffic.bytes | head 5
```

**Supplier IP ranges lookup:**
Place `nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-supply/lookups/`. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[nis2_supplier_ip_ranges]
filename = nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv
match_type = CIDR(cidr)
```
The `match_type = CIDR(cidr)` enables CIDR matching — a query IP of "10.200.0.15" will match the CIDR "10.200.0.0/24".

Validate: `| inputlookup nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv | stats count dc(supplier_name) as suppliers dc(cidr) as ranges values(supplier_tier) as tiers`.

**Test CIDR matching:**
```
| makeresults | eval src="10.200.0.15" | lookup nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv cidr AS src OUTPUT supplier_name
```
Expected: `supplier_name` populated if 10.200.0.15 falls within a registered CIDR range.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.2 NIS2 Supply Chain Network Monitoring`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **Traffic enrichment via CIDR lookup**: `lookup nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv cidr AS src_ip OUTPUT supplier_name supplier_tier allowed_ports | where isnotnull(supplier_name)` identifies traffic originating from or destined to supplier IP ranges. Only traffic involving a registered supplier produces results.

• **Baseline comparison**: the SPL calculates a 30-day baseline of daily bytes per supplier using `stats avg(daily_bytes) as baseline_bytes by supplier_name` for the 7-30 day window, then compares the current 7-day window's daily average against the baseline. `eval anomaly_ratio=round(current_bytes / baseline_bytes, 1)` calculates the ratio. `where anomaly_ratio > 2.0 OR anomaly_ratio < 0.3` flags both spikes (potential exfiltration) and drops (potential connectivity loss).

• **Unauthorised port detection**: `eval unauthorised_port=if(NOT match(dest_port, allowed_ports_regex), 1, 0)` identifies traffic on ports not explicitly allowed for the supplier. A supplier authorised for "443,8443" using port 3389 (RDP) is an anomaly.

• **Application identification (Palo Alto)**: if using a NGFW with application identification, `eval unauthorised_app=if(NOT match(app, allowed_apps_regex), 1, 0)` provides deeper inspection than port-based analysis.

Schedule: cron `0 7 * * *` (daily at 07:00). Time range: 30-day lookback for baseline, 7-day current window. Trigger: `anomaly_ratio > 2.0 OR unauthorised_port=1`.

Alert actions: (1) For traffic volume anomalies (anomaly_ratio > 3.0): PagerDuty page to the SOC — possible data exfiltration. (2) For unauthorised ports: email to the network security team. (3) For traffic drops (anomaly_ratio < 0.3): email to the supplier relationship manager — connectivity issue or decommissioned connection. (4) Write to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:supply_chain:traffic`.

Extended search — supplier traffic summary:
```spl
| tstats sum(All_Traffic.bytes) as bytes dc(All_Traffic.dest_port) as port_diversity from datamodel=Network_Traffic where earliest=-7d by All_Traffic.src
| rename All_Traffic.src as src_ip All_Traffic.bytes as bytes
| lookup nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv cidr AS src_ip OUTPUT supplier_name supplier_tier
| where isnotnull(supplier_name)
| stats sum(bytes) as total_bytes dc(src_ip) as source_ips max(port_diversity) as port_diversity by supplier_name supplier_tier
| eval total_gb=round(total_bytes/1073741824, 2)
| sort - total_bytes
| table supplier_name supplier_tier source_ips total_gb port_diversity
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Supplier traffic identification**: verify that traffic from a known supplier IP is correctly enriched with `supplier_name`. Run: `index=firewall src_ip=10.200.0.15 earliest=-1h | lookup nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv cidr AS src_ip OUTPUT supplier_name | stats count by supplier_name`.

(b) **Anomaly detection**: artificially inflate the current window by ingesting test events with high byte counts. Verify the anomaly ratio exceeds the threshold.

(c) **Unauthorised port detection**: identify a supplier connection using a port not in `allowed_ports`. Verify it appears as `unauthorised_port=1`.

(d) **Baseline stability**: for a supplier with consistent traffic, verify `anomaly_ratio` is between 0.8 and 1.2 (normal range).

(e) **CIDR coverage**: `index=firewall earliest=-7d | lookup nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv cidr AS src_ip OUTPUT supplier_name | stats count(eval(isnotnull(supplier_name))) as tagged count as total | eval coverage_pct=round(100*tagged/total,1)`. Low coverage may indicate missing supplier IP ranges in the CSV.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Supply Chain Network`):
• **Row 1 — Traffic posture**: Single-value `Suppliers monitored`. Single-value `Traffic anomalies (7d)` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Unauthorised port detections`. Single-value `Total supplier traffic (GB)`.
• **Row 2 — Supplier traffic ranking**: table from extended search. Highlight suppliers with high port diversity (may indicate port scanning).
• **Row 3 — Anomaly timeline**: timechart of bytes by supplier for the last 30 days. Overlay the baseline as a reference line.
• **Row 4 — Unauthorised port detail**: table of `supplier_name`, `src_ip`, `dest_ip`, `dest_port`, `bytes`, `_time`. Drill-down for investigation.

Runbook (owner: SOC / Procurement):
1. Daily: review anomalies. For volume spikes: (a) check the supplier's change calendar — is a data migration or software deployment underway? (b) Contact the supplier if unexplained. (c) If data exfiltration is suspected, escalate to the IR team.
2. Weekly: review unauthorised port detections. Work with the network team to verify whether the port should be added to `allowed_ports` (legitimate business need) or blocked (unauthorised access).
3. Monthly: review the supplier traffic summary with procurement. Identify suppliers with unusual patterns.
4. Quarterly: archive as part of the Art.21(2)(d) evidence package.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **CIDR lookup returns null for known supplier IPs.** The `match_type = CIDR(cidr)` requires exact column name matching. If the CSV has extra whitespace or BOM characters, the lookup fails silently. Fix: re-save the CSV in UTF-8 without BOM. Verify with `| inputlookup nis2_supplier_ip_ranges.csv | head 1 | eval hex=tostring(cidr,"hex")` — check for unexpected leading bytes.

• **NAT obscures supplier source IPs.** If supplier traffic passes through a NAT device, the source IP seen by the firewall is the NAT IP, not the supplier's real IP. Fix: register the NAT IP in the CSV. Or use the Palo Alto `X-Forwarded-For` field if available.

• **Baseline period includes a previous anomaly.** If a data exfiltration event occurred 20 days ago, it's included in the 30-day baseline, raising the baseline and potentially masking the current anomaly. Fix: use a rolling median instead of mean for the baseline. `perc50(daily_bytes)` is more robust to outliers than `avg(daily_bytes)`.

• **Too many false positives from allowed applications.** Legitimate application updates (e.g., Windows Update via a cloud supplier) cause regular traffic spikes. Fix: exclude known update windows with a `| lookup supplier_maintenance_windows.csv supplier_name OUTPUT maintenance_start maintenance_end | where NOT (now() > maintenance_start AND now() < maintenance_end)`.

• **tstats performance on large firewall indexes.** If `index=firewall` contains billions of events, `tstats` queries may be slow. Fix: ensure the Network_Traffic data model is accelerated: `| datamodel Network_Traffic | accelerate`. Verify acceleration status in Settings > Data Models.
grandmaExplanation: We watch who connects into outside companies that keep your critical systems running, and we notice when those connections line up with known bad internet names or when someone stays logged in far longer than normal.
